summary: Dr. Graham A. Colditz emphasizes the urgent need to shift focus toward breast cancer prevention in low and middle-income countries. He argues that economic transitions in these regions will inevitably increase breast cancer rates, and relying solely on diagnosis and treatment is economically unsustainable.

principles.statement: Global health efforts must prioritize breast cancer prevention in low and middle-income countries.
principles.rationale: As these countries undergo economic transitions, breast cancer rates will rise. They lack the financial resources to rely solely on diagnosing and treating the disease.
principles.source_quote: we can't afford to diagnose and pre breast cancer in Low Middle income countries and it's one area we don't currently have a robust prevention focus

mental_models.name: Economic Transition Disease Shift
mental_models.description: The understanding that as developing nations undergo economic transition, changes in demographics and lifestyle (e.g., fewer children, better nutrition, earlier onset of menstrual periods) directly lead to an increased incidence of specific diseases like breast cancer.
mental_models.example: Predicting that the percentage of breast cancer in women will rise in low and middle-income countries as they develop economically.

signature_quotes.text: we can't afford to diagnose and pre breast cancer in Low Middle income countries and it's one area we don't currently have a robust prevention focus
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the economic realities of cancer care in developing nations.

signature_quotes.text: a quarter of all cancer diagnosed in women at the moment is breast cancer and that percentage will go up as countries go through the economic transition
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the impact of economic development on global cancer rates.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on diagnosis and treatment as the primary strategy for managing breast cancer in developing nations.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Low and middle-income countries cannot afford the financial burden of diagnosing and treating the disease at scale, making a lack of prevention economically disastrous.
